name: Diversify Platform Integrity Signals
consequences: Pulling this lever expands the set of device integrity checks to include open‑source OS attestation and hardware root‑of‑trust modules, reducing reliance on Apple/Google APIs. This creates a more resilient trust chain but increases complexity for developers and may slow adoption of new devices. It also opens a path for third‑party attestation providers, potentially raising regulatory scrutiny.
options: Integrate GrapheneOS attestation into the MitID verification flow to provide an open‑source integrity signal.
Deploy a hardware root‑of‑trust module that issues signed device certificates independent of mobile OS.
Leverage a community‑driven attestation service that aggregates device integrity data from multiple vendors.
review: This lever addresses the tension between centralized OS‑based integrity and distributed trust, but the options overlook the need for cross‑platform compatibility testing.

name: Design a Platform‑Neutral Fallback Path
consequences: Implementing a fallback path allows citizens to authenticate via web or hardware token when mobile OS integrity signals are unavailable. This enhances continuity but requires additional user onboarding steps and may reduce perceived convenience. It also demands coordination with existing identity providers to accept alternative credentials.
options: Build a browser‑based WebAuthn portal that accepts FIDO2 credentials from any device.
Create a USB security key distribution program that pre‑registers keys with the national identity provider.
Offer a QR‑code based one‑time code system that works on any smartphone, bypassing OS integrity checks.
review: This lever balances resilience against user convenience, yet the options fail to address the cost of provisioning and managing fallback devices.

name: Embed Platform‑Neutral Clauses in Procurement
consequences: Adding explicit platform‑neutral requirements to procurement documents forces vendors to design solutions that do not rely on Apple/Google. This can slow the procurement cycle and increase upfront costs but ultimately reduces long‑term dependency. It also signals to the market that Denmark prioritizes sovereignty.
options: Require all eIDAS2 wallet submissions to include a documented fallback authentication mechanism.
Mandate that mobile app distribution must support an open‑source app store channel.
Insist that vendor contracts include a clause for periodic audit of platform dependency levels.
review: This lever tackles the tension between procurement speed and sovereignty, but the options ignore potential legal challenges to mandatory clauses.

name: Mobilize a Cross‑Sector Coalition
consequences: Building a coalition of civil society, academia, and industry amplifies advocacy pressure on policymakers. It can accelerate policy adoption but may dilute focus if coalition members have conflicting priorities. It also requires sustained coordination effort.
options: Form a formal working group with representatives from privacy NGOs, universities, and tech firms to draft joint policy briefs.
Launch a public awareness campaign highlighting platform lock‑in risks through targeted media events.
Create a shared online platform where stakeholders can contribute evidence and track policy progress.
review: This lever confronts the tension between broad stakeholder engagement and focused action, yet the options overlook the risk of coalition fatigue.

name: Align with EU Digital Identity Standards
consequences: Aligning the Danish fallback strategy with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards increases interoperability and may attract EU funding. It may also impose constraints that limit domestic flexibility. It signals Denmark’s commitment to European integration.
options: Submit a technical contribution to the EU Digital Identity Wallet working group proposing platform‑neutral fallback modules.
Adopt the EU’s OpenID Connect profile for fallback authentication and map it to national requirements.
Participate in a joint EU‑Nordic research project to develop cross‑border fallback solutions.
review: This lever addresses the tension between national sovereignty and European integration, but the options miss the challenge of aligning divergent national procurement cycles.

name: Frame Resilience as a Public Service Imperative
consequences: Positioning platform dependency as a continuity risk reframes the debate from privacy to national security. This can mobilize political support but may also provoke backlash from tech incumbents. It may also shift public perception of digital identity services.
options: Publish a risk assessment report linking platform lock‑in to potential service outages during geopolitical tensions.
Host a roundtable with defense and emergency management agencies to discuss identity resilience.
Develop a public‑facing dashboard that visualizes dependency metrics for each major platform.
review: This lever balances security framing against political resistance, yet the options fail to consider the cost of maintaining a continuous risk communication channel.

name: Reframe Digital Identity Procurement Criteria
consequences: Pulling this lever changes procurement documents to require platform neutrality, reducing reliance on Apple and Google. This may increase vendor selection complexity and require new evaluation criteria, potentially slowing procurement cycles.
options: Mandate that all future digital identity contracts include a clause obligating vendors to provide platform-neutral authentication pathways, thereby preventing exclusive reliance on foreign OS ecosystems.
Introduce a scoring rubric that rewards vendors for demonstrating cross-platform compatibility and fallback mechanisms, encouraging the development of diversified authentication solutions.
Require that procurement specifications explicitly forbid the use of platform integrity signals as the sole trust anchor, compelling vendors to adopt open standards.
review: This lever addresses the tension between procurement flexibility and platform dependency, but the options ignore the potential for increased administrative burden on small vendors.

name: Institutionalize Multi-Platform Authentication Standards
consequences: Pulling this lever leads to formal adoption of standards that allow authentication across multiple OS, reducing lock-in. It may require training, system changes, and potential compatibility issues.
options: Publish a national reference architecture that defines interoperable authentication protocols, enabling service providers to integrate multiple operating systems without compromising security.
Establish a certification program for authentication solutions that prove compliance with cross-platform standards, incentivizing vendors to support diverse device ecosystems.
Mandate that all new identity services undergo a multi-platform compatibility audit before approval, ensuring that fallback authentication paths are viable across all major OS.
review: This lever tackles the tension between standardization and innovation, yet the options fail to address how legacy systems will transition to the new multi-platform framework.

name: Embed Resilience Metrics into Public Service Contracts
consequences: Pulling this lever embeds resilience KPIs into contracts, forcing vendors to meet continuity standards. It may increase cost and require monitoring, but improves resilience.
options: Insert a clause that requires vendors to report quarterly uptime and incident response times for authentication services, thereby quantifying resilience performance.
Define a resilience scorecard that evaluates vendors on their ability to maintain authentication availability during platform outages, and tie contract bonuses to high scores.
Require that all service level agreements include a fallback authentication provision that must be tested annually, ensuring continuity in the event of platform disruptions.
review: This lever balances resilience with contractual oversight, but the options overlook the challenge of enforcing compliance across multiple independent vendors.

name: Leverage Academic-Industry Partnerships for Platform Neutrality
consequences: Pulling this lever fosters collaboration between academia and industry to develop platform-neutral solutions. It may accelerate innovation but requires coordination and intellectual property management.
options: Create a joint research grant that funds interdisciplinary teams to prototype platform-neutral authentication modules, ensuring that academic insights translate into industry-ready designs.
Establish a public-private innovation hub that hosts hackathons focused on building fallback authentication solutions, thereby crowdsourcing diverse technical approaches.
Launch a scholarship program for students specializing in secure authentication, encouraging them to pursue research that supports platform-neutral deployment in public services.
review: This lever addresses the tension between academic research and industry uptake, yet the options miss how to align intellectual property rights with public procurement needs.

name: Catalyze Public-Private Dialogue on Supplier Concentration
consequences: Pulling this lever initiates structured dialogue to reduce supplier concentration. It may lead to policy changes but could face political resistance.
options: Organize a quarterly roundtable that brings together government officials, platform vendors, and civil society to openly discuss the risks of supplier concentration and explore mitigation strategies.
Publish a white paper that quantifies the economic impact of platform lock-in on public services, using the findings to lobby for diversified procurement policies.
Launch a public consultation campaign that invites stakeholders to propose alternative authentication architectures, thereby democratizing the design of future identity systems.
review: This lever confronts the tension between market dominance and policy diversification, but the options overlook how to sustain engagement beyond initial meetings.

name: Integrate Contingency Planning into National Digital Identity Roadmap
consequences: Pulling this lever ensures contingency plans are embedded in the roadmap, improving resilience. It may require additional resources but provides clear fallback paths.
options: Embed a dedicated contingency module in the national roadmap that outlines step-by-step procedures for switching to alternative authentication providers during platform disruptions.
Mandate that all roadmap milestones include a risk assessment of platform dependency, ensuring that fallback options are revisited at each planning cycle.
Create a cross-functional task force that monitors emerging platform threats and updates contingency protocols in real time, keeping the roadmap responsive.
review: This lever addresses the tension between long-term planning and rapid threat response, yet the options ignore how to integrate contingency updates into existing procurement cycles.

name: Reconfigure AltID Procurement Language
consequences: Changing procurement language to mandate platform neutrality will force vendors to design solutions without relying on Apple/Google signals. This may slow initial vendor onboarding but increases long‑term resilience and reduces supplier concentration risk. It also signals to EU bodies that Denmark prioritizes sovereign digital identity.
options: Draft a procurement clause that requires all future AltID components to certify platform‑neutral authentication pathways before approval.
Introduce a mandatory audit of existing AltID modules to verify absence of platform‑dependent integrity checks, with penalties for non‑compliance.
Mandate that any new public‑sector identity service must publish a public technical specification demonstrating independence from mobile OS integrity APIs.
review: This lever addresses the tension between procurement flexibility and sovereign resilience, but the options ignore the potential delay in vendor selection and the need for interim service continuity.

name: Institutionalize Cross‑Agency Resilience Audits
consequences: Regular audits across ministries will surface hidden dependencies on platform signals and expose gaps in fallback readiness. The audits will create a shared risk register, encouraging agencies to collaborate on mitigation. However, audit cycles may strain agency resources and risk politicization of findings.
options: Establish a quarterly resilience audit committee that reviews each agency’s identity access stack for platform dependencies and publishes a joint risk report.
Require agencies to submit a platform‑dependency matrix as part of their annual IT budget, with a compliance score that influences funding allocations.
Create a shared digital resilience dashboard that aggregates audit findings and tracks progress toward platform neutrality across all ministries.
review: This lever balances transparency with bureaucratic burden, yet the options fail to address how to enforce corrective actions once dependencies are identified.

name: Embed Platform‑Neutral Signaling in Public Service APIs
consequences: Adding platform‑neutral authentication signals to public APIs will decouple citizen access from mobile OS trust anchors. It will broaden device compatibility and reduce vendor lock‑in. The change may require significant API redesign and developer retraining.
options: Introduce a new authentication header that accepts FIDO2/WebAuthn assertions, allowing any compliant device to authenticate without OS‑specific signals.
Replace existing push‑notification authentication flows with a server‑initiated challenge that operates over HTTPS, eliminating reliance on platform push services.
Deploy a middleware gateway that translates platform‑neutral tokens into legacy API calls, enabling backward compatibility while gradually phasing out OS dependencies.
review: This lever tackles the tension between API stability and platform independence, but the options overlook the cost of widespread developer migration and potential security gaps during transition.

name: Establish Dedicated Fallback Authentication Pilot
consequences: A pilot demonstrating fallback authentication will provide tangible evidence that non‑platform‑dependent access is feasible. It will build stakeholder confidence and supply data for procurement language. The pilot may divert resources from other project phases and risk public perception if it fails.
options: Build a low‑cost hardware‑token prototype that citizens can use to authenticate to public services when mobile OS integrity is unavailable.
Deploy a browser‑based fallback that leverages WebAuthn and a secure enclave on the server to authenticate users without mobile OS signals.
Create a temporary mobile app that operates on a privacy‑focused OS like GrapheneOS, demonstrating end‑to‑end authentication without Apple/Google integrity APIs.
review: This lever addresses the need for proof of concept versus operational readiness, yet the options do not consider how to scale the pilot into a national rollout.

name: Leverage EU Digital Identity Standards for National Policy
consequences: Aligning national policy with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards will embed platform neutrality into broader regulatory frameworks. It will enhance cross‑border interoperability and reduce Denmark’s reliance on foreign platforms. The alignment effort may slow domestic policy cycles and require coordination with multiple EU bodies.
options: Submit a formal position paper to the European Digital Identity Working Group advocating mandatory fallback authentication in all member‑state identity wallets.
Participate in EU standardization meetings to co‑author a technical specification that defines platform‑neutral trust anchors for eIDAS‑compliant services.
Organize a national EU‑level workshop that brings together Danish stakeholders and EU regulators to co‑create a roadmap for platform‑neutral identity deployment.
review: This lever balances national sovereignty with EU integration, but the options overlook the potential for divergent EU member‑state priorities to dilute Denmark’s influence.

name: Create Independent Oversight Board for Platform Dependence
consequences: An independent board would monitor platform dependence across public services, ensuring accountability and transparency. It would provide an external audit mechanism and a forum for stakeholder input. The board could face political resistance and may lack enforcement power without legislative backing.
options: Establish a statutory oversight committee composed of civil‑society experts, technologists, and policymakers to review platform‑dependency metrics quarterly.
Create a public registry that lists all identity‑related services and their platform‑dependency status, updated by the oversight board.
Require the oversight board to issue annual certification for services that meet platform‑neutral criteria, with public disclosure of non‑compliant services.
review: This lever confronts the tension between independent scrutiny and political influence, yet the options miss how to secure the board’s authority to compel changes.
